content:
# Genesis Chapter 47 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27v–28r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_047_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 47–48

---

*Joseph Presents His Brothers to Pharaoh — Goshen Granted (vv. 1–6)*

**47:1** And Joseph came and told Pharaoh, and said, "My father and my brothers, and their flocks and their herds and all that they have, have come from the land of Canaan; and behold, they are in the land of Goshen."

**47:2** And from among his brothers he took five men and presented them before Pharaoh.

**47:3** And Pharaoh said to his brothers, "What is your occupation?" And they said to Pharaoh, "Your servants are shepherds of flocks, both we and our fathers."

**47:4** And they said to Pharaoh, "We have come to sojourn in the land, for there is no pasture for your servants' flocks, for the famine is severe in the land of Canaan. And now, please let your servants dwell in the land of Goshen."

**47:5** Then Pharaoh spoke to Joseph, saying, "Your father and your brothers have come to you.

**47:6** The land of Egypt is before you. In the best of the land settle your father and your brothers; let them dwell in the land of Goshen. And if you know any capable men among them, put them in charge of my livestock."

---

*Jacob Blesses Pharaoh — "Few and Evil" (vv. 7–10)*

**47:7** Then Joseph brought in Jacob his father and presented him before Pharaoh, and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.

**47:8** And Pharaoh said to Jacob, "How many are the days of the years of your life?"

**47:9** And Jacob said to Pharaoh, "The days of the years of my sojournings are one hundred and thirty years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not attained to the days of the years of the life of my fathers in the days of their sojournings."

**47:10** And Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh.

---

*Joseph Settles His Family in the Land of Rameses (vv. 11–12)*

**47:11** And Joseph settled his father and his brothers and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.

**47:12** And Joseph provided for his father and his brothers and all his father's household with bread, according to the number of their dependents.

---

*Joseph's Famine Administration — All Egypt for Pharaoh (vv. 13–26)*

**47:13** Now there was no bread in all the land, for the famine was very severe, and the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan languished because of the famine.

**47:14** And Joseph gathered up all the money that was found in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan, in exchange for the grain that they were buying, and Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh's house.

**47:15** And when the money was exhausted from the land of Egypt and from the land of Canaan, all the Egyptians came to Joseph and said, "Give us bread! Why should we die before your eyes? For the money is gone."

**47:16** And Joseph said, "Give your livestock, and I will give you bread in exchange for your livestock, if the money is gone."

**47:17** So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for the horses, and for the flocks, and for the herds, and for the donkeys. And he provided them with bread in exchange for all their livestock that year.

**47:18** And when that year ended, they came to him the following year and said to him, "We will not hide from my lord that the money is all spent, and the herds of livestock belong to my lord; there is nothing left before my lord but our bodies and our soil.

**47:19** Why should we die before your eyes, both we and our soil? Buy us and our soil for bread, and we and our soil will be servants to Pharaoh. And give us seed, that we may live and not die, and that the soil may not become desolate."

**47:20** So Joseph bought all the soil of Egypt for Pharaoh, for every Egyptian sold his field, because the famine was severe upon them. And the land became Pharaoh's.

**47:21** And as for the people, he moved them to the cities, from one end of Egypt's territory to the other.

**47:22** Only the soil of the priests he did not buy, for the priests had a fixed allotment from Pharaoh, and they ate their allotment that Pharaoh gave them; therefore they did not sell their soil.

**47:23** Then Joseph said to the people, "Behold, I have this day bought you and your soil for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you, and you shall sow the soil.

**47:24** And at the harvests you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh, and four parts shall be your own — for seed for the field, and for your food, and for those in your households, and as food for your little ones."

**47:25** And they said, "You have saved our lives! May we find favor in the eyes of my lord, and we will be servants to Pharaoh."

**47:26** So Joseph made it a statute to this day over the soil of Egypt, that Pharaoh should have the fifth — only the soil of the priests alone did not become Pharaoh's.

---

*Israel Fruitful in Goshen — Jacob's Final Request (vv. 27–31)*

**47:27** And Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the land of Goshen, and they acquired property in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly.

**47:28** And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years, so the days of Jacob, the years of his life, were one hundred and forty-seven years.

**47:29** And when the days of Israel drew near to die, he called his son Joseph and said to him, "If now I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and deal with me in covenant-faithfulness and truth: please do not bury me in Egypt.

**47:30** When I lie down with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place." And he said, "I will do as you have spoken."

**47:31** And he said, "Swear to me." And he swore to him. And Israel bowed himself at the head of the bed.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "From among his brothers" — miqtseh echav (v. 2): LOGOS upheld**

The Hebrew miqtseh echav ("from the end/extremity of his brothers") is genuinely ambiguous. EMET's "from the end of his brothers" is too literal — English readers cannot parse the spatial metaphor. PNEUMA's "from the full complement" resolves the ambiguity in one direction. LOGOS's "from among" is the most neutral rendering: it conveys selection without forcing an interpretation. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**2. "Money" — kesef (vv. 14, 15, 16, 18): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

Hebrew kesef means both "silver" and "money." In the famine-economy narrative, kesef functions as the medium of exchange — it is spent, exhausted, and gone. The narrative tracks sequential economic loss: first money, then livestock, then soil. "Money" serves this narrative arc more clearly than "silver," which in modern English emphasizes material composition rather than economic function. EMET's "silver" is more literal but less communicative.

**3. "According to the number of their dependents" — lefi ha-taf (v. 12): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET's "according to the mouth of the little ones" preserves the Hebrew but is opaque in English. The idiom means "proportional to household size." "Dependents" captures the meaning of taf as a synecdoche for the whole dependent household.

**4. "Soil" for adamah — (vv. 18, 19, 20, 22, 23, 26): EMET upheld**

The Hebrew uses two distinct nouns: erets ("land, country, territory") and adamah ("soil, cultivatable ground"). The Egyptians sell their adamah — their arable soil — to Pharaoh. The distinction matters: adamah is the word from which Adam is formed (Gen 2:7); it is the earth that bears crops. In v. 20, both words appear in the same verse: "Joseph bought all the soil (adamah) of Egypt... and the land (erets) became Pharaoh's." The first is the physical cultivated ground being purchased; the second is the political territory that results. Conflating them as "land" loses this precision. SOPHIA follows EMET: "soil" for adamah, "land" for erets.

**5. "Have not attained to" — hissigu (v. 9): LOGOS upheld**

The hiphil of nasag means "to reach, overtake, attain." EMET's "reached" is accurate but flat. LOGOS's "attained to" preserves the aspiration-and-falling-short nuance. PNEUMA's "matched" shifts the metaphor from reaching a target to comparison between equals — a different image.

**6. "When I lie down with my fathers" — veshakhavti (v. 30): PNEUMA upheld**

The weqatal veshakhavti follows the negative imperative "do not bury me in Egypt," functioning as a continuation: "Do not bury me in Egypt — when I lie down with my fathers, carry me out." PNEUMA's temporal rendering captures the logical sequence of Jacob's instructions. EMET's bare future disconnects from the imperative chain. LOGOS's "let me lie" adds a volitional mood not in the form.

**7. "Israel bowed himself" — vayishtachu (v. 31): LOGOS upheld**

The Hishtaphel of shachah can denote worship or obeisance — the ambiguity is deliberate. EMET's "bowed down" loses the reflexive. PNEUMA's "bowed in worship" resolves the ambiguity prematurely. LOGOS's "bowed himself" preserves both the reflexive form and the open question of whether Jacob worships God or bows toward Joseph.

**8. Full agreements (3-0):**

- "Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life" (v. 9) — all three preserve the raw force of me'at vere'im without softening.
- "The days of the years of my sojournings" (v. 9) — all three render megurai as "sojournings."
- "Covenant-faithfulness and truth" for chesed ve-emet (v. 29) — project convention honored by all three.
- "Put your hand under my thigh" (v. 29) — all three render the oath gesture literally.
- "At the head of the bed" (v. 31) — all three follow MT (mittah/bed) against LXX (matteh/staff).
- "He moved them to the cities" (v. 21) — all three follow MT (he'evir/moved) against LXX (katadoulosato/enslaved).
- "Were fruitful and multiplied greatly" (v. 27) — all three preserve the creation-mandate language.
- Jacob blesses Pharaoh twice (vv. 7, 10) — all three render barakh without commentary.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 47–48)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 47:2 | "from the end of his brothers" (miqtseh echav) | "from some of his brothers" (ἀπὸ τῶν ἀδελφῶν) | Near-equivalent; both support "from among" |
| 47:9 | "few and evil" (me'at vere'im) | "few and evil" (ὀλίγαι καὶ πονηραί) | Agreement; both preserve full force |
| 47:11 | "the land of Rameses" (erets Ra'meses) | "the land of Rameses" (γῆ Ραμεσση) | Agreement on place name |
| 47:12 | "according to the mouth of the little ones" (lefi ha-taf) | "according to the body/person" (κατὰ σῶμα) | LXX interprets differently — "per person" rather than "per dependent" |
| 47:21 | "he moved them to the cities" (he'evir otam le'arim) | "he enslaved them as servants" (κατεδουλώσατο αὐτούς) | Follow MT: the harder reading. LXX reflects Samaritan le'avadim ("slaves") not MT le'arim ("cities") |
| 47:22 | "a fixed allotment" (choq) | "a gift" (δόσις) | Follow MT: choq is a statutory allocation, not a discretionary gift |
| 47:27 | "were fruitful and multiplied greatly" | "grew and multiplied very much" (ηὐξήθησαν καὶ ἐπληθύνθησαν) | Near-equivalent; different Greek verbs, same emphasis |
| 47:29 | "covenant-faithfulness and truth" (chesed ve-emet) | "mercy and truth" (ἐλεημοσύνην καὶ ἀλήθειαν) | Follow MT: chesed is covenant loyalty, not merely mercy |
| 47:31 | "at the head of the bed" (al-rosh ha-mittah) | "at the top of his staff" (ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ) | Follow MT: mittah (bed) fits the immediate context — Jacob is ill (48:1). The LXX reads matteh (staff); Heb 11:21 follows LXX |
| 47:31 | "bowed himself" (vayishtachu) | "worshiped" (προσεκύνησεν) | Near-equivalent: LXX resolves toward worship; MT preserves ambiguity |

---

### The Bed or the Staff — A Note on Hebrews 11:21

The consonantal text מטה can be vocalized as mittah ("bed") or matteh ("staff"). The Masoretes chose mittah; the LXX translator read matteh. Both produce coherent images:

- **MT (bed):** Jacob, ill and bedridden (cf. 48:1), turns to bow at the headboard — worship from a position of physical weakness. The dying patriarch who cannot rise still bows before God.
- **LXX (staff):** Jacob, leaning on his shepherd's staff, bows in dignity — the pilgrim patriarch worshiping on his feet to the last. The staff connects to Jacob's own words at the Jabbok: "with my staff I crossed this Jordan" (Gen 32:10).

Hebrews 11:21 follows the LXX: "By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, bowing in worship over the top of his staff." The NT author reads 47:31 and 48 as one continuous deathbed act of faith. Neither reading is a corruption; both arise from the same unpointed consonants. This translation follows MT while acknowledging the canonical status of the LXX reading in the Christian tradition.

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
